[linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/company/european-pharmaceutical-manufacturer/) [RSS](https://pharmaceuticalmanufacturer.media/api/rss/content.rss)

[![European Pharmaceutical Manufacturer](https://pharmaceuticalmanufacturer.media/api/design-015f00cd7a2167741929e2d8d0007d7d/epm_masthead2021-01.png)![European Pharmaceutical Manufacturer](https://pharmaceuticalmanufacturer.media/api/design-015f00cd7a2167741929e2d8d0007d7d/epm_masthead2018mobile.png)](https://pharmaceuticalmanufacturer.media/ "European Pharmaceutical Manufacturer")

**The pharmaceutical industry continues to evolve, adopting new technologies and optimising resources and expertise to meet authentication and anti-counterfeiting challenges, says Dr Mark Deakes, chairman of the International Optical Technologies Association (IOTA).**

×

Expand

![shutterstock_1202705410.jpg](https://pharmaceuticalmanufacturer.media/downloads/12597/download/shutterstock_1202705410.jpg?cb=6332c0beb9ae21343e7406bf4a8f9805&w={width}&h={height})

SOLDATOOFF Shutterstock

Global prescription drug sales are predicted to reach $1.6 trillion by 2028, according to one industry report. Other industry analysis forecasts a CAGR of 6.12% between 2025 and 2030, reaching $2,350.43 billion by 2030. Growth is being driven by increasing global healthcare demands and advancements in obesity, neurological conditions, and cancer treatment among other factors.

However, while new technologies propel global advances, the pharmaceutical industry continues to struggle to combat the threat from organised criminals and opportunist counterfeiters. Indeed, pharmaceuticals is among the world’s largest fraud markets, if not the largest.

The international trade in illicit or unauthorised pharmaceuticals is a very real and present danger to public health programmes. For example, according to Europol, over €11m worth of fake medicines were taken off markets in late 2024 across the Eurozone, while multinational pharmaceutical manufacturer Pfizer says counterfeit prescription medicines have become a $200bn illicit global business annually. The types of drugs being sold range from chronic medications for diabetes and heart disease to cancer drugs and antiretrovirals for HIV. At the same time, counterfeit prescription medications laced with fentanyl are being sold on the internet and social media, causing an uptake in fatal overdoses across the US, says Pfizer. We are also seeing counterfeiting activity continue to be abetted by compromises in global disruption networks, poor law enforcement in some regions, regulatory uncertainties and unprecedented demand for critical drugs and medical products.

The distribution of counterfeit pharmaceutical products, which include fake anti-cancer drugs, analgesics, antioestrogens, antivirals, anabolic substances and in recent times, Covid kits, continues to shift from the physical to online markets, particularly via the dark web, where anonymous transactions between manufacturers, distributors and consumers are allowed. The number of unauthorised and unregulated online outlets selling counterfeit pharmaceutical products grows annually, with many selling counterfeit medications via the internet typically posing as legitimate vendors.

Furthermore, as well as causing harm to people, pharmaceutical-related crime damages legitimate manufacturers, who are impacted by unsafe counterfeits that can erode brand reputation and reduce the funds available for legitimate new research and investment in product development.

Tackling pharmaceutical counterfeiting and brand piracy is therefore of paramount concern for manufacturers, healthcare authorities and law enforcement - and one area in which the problem can be tackled effectively is product packaging featuring optical security devices such as micro-lenses, micro-mirrors, colour shifting, print, diffractive and holographic technologies.

New trends in pharmaceutical packaging follow developments in emerging markets, demographic changes and new technologies, and with _estimates that t_ he total global value for tracking, tamper-evidence and product authentication in 2025 will reach US $4.0 billion _, g_ rowth in authentication and anti-tamper devices appears to have a healthy future. This comes on the back of a rise in wider global healthcare concerns, higher adoption rates of anti-counterfeit technologies and greater awareness of the benefits of new track and trace technologies and system integration.

#### Combatting counterfeiting global examples

Packaging featuring security devices can ensure quality and thwart the distribution and smuggling of illicit products, while items not displaying optical security devices can be quickly seized and destroyed. Today, the advances in application, film coating and manufacturing technology are rolling back the boundaries for the use of a new technologies, facilitating fresh levels of visual effect, brand enhancement, regulatory compliance and anti-counterfeiting.

While some major manufacturing countries in Asia, continue to struggle with counterfeit medicines, Malaysia and Italy are good examples of countries that have adopted successful security pharmaceutical label programmes to serve the public interest effectively.

Malaysia became the first country to implement a nationwide holographic label anticounterfeit program for pharmaceuticals 20 years ago. This initiative, led by the Pharmaceutical Services Department under the Ministry of Health, has become one of the world’s longest-running and most effective anti-counterfeit medicine programmes and has consistently demonstrated success while other nations are also bringing forward their own initiatives. Developed by the Istituto Poligrafico e Zecca dello Stato (IPZS) in Italy, the IPZS Bollino security label, for example, which features optical elements and a barcode for enhanced security, illustrates how national printing authorities can innovate.

Other pharmaceutical organisations approach authentication with the same attention to a mix of brand identity and anti-counterfeiting features. In China, Gilead’s EPCLUSA and TRODELVY medicines carry tamper-evident seals on both the top and bottom of their carton packaging, with the top seal comprising a colour shifting hologram and variable QR code. When the hologram is tilted strongly forward, the image of the small ‘Leaf and Shield’ logo and ‘Gilead’ brand name appear bright green against a dark larger logo. In the case of the Gilead labels, when the top label is removed, the hologram will split into an ‘X’ to indicate the box has been opened, whereas the QR code remains on the box.

In addition, the label can be checked by scanning the QR code with a smartphone, which directs the user to a Gilead response page, notifying them if the QR code is valid. As for the label on the bottom of the medicine packaging, this has a ‘lock’ VOID effect. When the label is peeled off, two opened lock images appear. This effect is irreversible, i.e. the locks remain visible even when trying to reseal the label. Gilead also uses similar solutions for its premium Veklury (remdesivir) brand, which treats patients for COVID-19.

Multinational pharmaceutical, biotechnology, and medical technologies corporation Johnson & Johnson uses a range of authentication solutions to prevent counterfeiting and ensure the authenticity of its products. One example is its Janssen COVID-19 vaccine box and vial labels. While the box label carries a tamper-evident feature along with a datamatrix and QR code, the vials inside the box carry a datamatrix. The company also applies similar anticounterfeiting measures across its portfolio of high-risk products, including cancer treatments and medical devices. In 2022, J&J announced a significant milestone, completing a nearly decade-long effort to embed unique device identifiers (UDIs) into the barcodes of 70,000 different medical devices it sells in the United States – becoming one of the first healthcare companies in the world to do so. It is now focusing on expanding its UDI efforts in other countries.

In Japan Chugai Pharmaceutical, one of country’s top five pharma companies has intensified its efforts to protect patients and ensure the authenticity of its products, by implementing an anti-counterfeit strategy which includes the application of tamperproof tape and security holograms on product packaging. This augments the mandated use of a serialised traceability code on pharma products, in the form of a 2D data matrix code, to help the supply chain and law enforcement differentiate between counterfeit/adulterated and genuine/intact products.

One of India’s largest pharmaceutical companies, Mankind Pharma, sees the value of holograms and security labels embedded with advanced anti-counterfeiting features (3D holographic images, unique patterns and microtext) as the most difficult to replicate. Similarly, the use of blockchain improves traceability via end-to-end tracking of shipments, helping to curb counterfeit drugs in the Indian pharmaceutical industry, which is projected to reach $130 billion by 2030 as the country stands poised to become the ‘world’s pharmacy’.

Elements such as QR codes, holograms, and security labels help to verify the authenticity of products as India’s pharmaceutical companies invest in secure, distinctive, difficult-to-copy packaging that is changed periodically to make counterfeiting challenging. The ability to combine with new emerging digital technologies such as QR codes, AI, and blockchain further confirms holography’s evolving role and ability to remain relevant as one of the most used overt physical authentication measures.

#### Collaboration

Technology collaboration is increasingly a key ingredient in delivering successful pharmaceutical anti-counterfeiting strategies. For example, the partnership between Crane Authentication & MM Packaging is integrating advanced micro-optics technology into pharmaceutical packaging. Originally developed for banknote security, this technology involve making tiny lenses on the packaging that create three-dimensional effects that can be customised with specific icons or designs.

For example, German security technology firm Giesecke+Devrient (G+D) has been working with security printing partners for several years in the product authentication market. Another example is Giesecke+Devrient (G+D) authentication solution, SIGN, which is based on micro-optic technology and security features are embossed directly onto the packaging. The technology has been used for over one billion packages in the pharmaceutical sector.

One of G+D’s other notable innovations is its entry into smart packaging and logistics with the release of its second-generation Smart Label, a paper-thin IoT device that transforms packages into intelligent, trackable items. Developed in collaboration with Sensos, this new Smart Label includes hardware, iSIM, cloud connectivity, and firmware updates. It offers GPS-enabled location accuracy within 10 metres, motion sensing, tamper detection, and temperature monitoring, making it ideal for thwarting counterfeiting activity in the global pharmaceutical supply chain.

Indeed, we are seeing optical security technologies embracing some interesting roles beyond traditional security and authentication. For instance, they can assist in maintaining the time and temperature of packaging, offering some distinct advantages, notably combined tampering, authentication and temperature monitoring all with in the same solutions’ package. Photonic crystal-based time temperature indicators change colour based on fluctuations in the optical properties of a photonic crystal structure, which is affected by the infiltration of a polymer due to temperature changes.

Optical security technology has advanced to such a position that when it’s integrated with track and trace it can provide manufacturers with the tools to be fully compliant with shifting legislative landscapes - incorporating beneficial features can help users generate unique sequential, encrypted or random serial numbers, or identify and mark products overtly or covertly either via special self-adhesive labels or directly onto pharmaceutical products using a variety of print technologies.

#### Expansive role

The ability of optical security technology to incorporate a broad range of data forms and product tracking information continues to be paramount as it pushes forward and the nature of anti-counterfeiting changes with the times. This enables micro-lenses, micro-mirrors, colour shifting, print, diffractive and holographic technologies to be used for an expansive range of pharmaceutical authentication and brand protection roles, linking on-pack product identification with supply chain management, market enforcement and forensic support services. In this way, the identity and distribution of goods can be controlled through a total system solution involving security authentication features, tracking mechanisms and investigative services.

For example, TOPPAN Holdings new colour version of its innovative Illumigram hologram. Originally available only in white, the enhanced version now presents multi-colour 3D text and images that respond to strong light sources, such as a smartphone torch. The background of Illumigram appears cloudy under sunlight or fluorescent light (left), but the word ‘GENUINE’ appears in two colours when exposed to a smartphone’s flashlight. Launched this April, the new colour Illumigram is targeted at sectors that face high risks of counterfeiting, including pharmaceuticals.

Another example of next-level optical security with 3D spatial effects is Kurz TRUSTCOLOR PLUS. TRUSTCOLOR PLUS takes colour-shifting authentication to the next level by combining it with a stunning 3D Spatial Effect (SFX). This advanced technology creates a sense of depth and movement, resulting in a fascinating optical feature that is nearly impossible to replicate. The combination of metallic colour flip and customised 3D effects makes it an eye-catching yet highly secure solution for brand protection.

On the OVI front, an example is SICPA QUAZAR, a product security label that includes tamper-evidence, secure 2D codes, as well as covert and forensic security features. It comes with a spectrum of colours and can be customised into aesthetically creative labels corresponding to the design specifications and colour precision for strategic product branding and protection.

Of notable value to the brand owner and a strong financial incentive to make the investment in such systems, is the fact that the information generated at the labelling stage can be linked to the company’s ERP (enterprise resource planning) system; enabling business functions such as manufacturing, supply chain management, finance, projects, HR and customer relationship management to be captured and better managed.

Moves towards outsourcing the production of pharmaceutical and healthcare products might be beneficial in terms of reducing manufacturing costs but it can also impact upon the control of brand security and visibility. Here, optical security devices are integrated into the supply chain security process to allow companies to maintain control of their products. They can also be combined with secure web interfaces to help eliminate the rogue ordering of products while authorised distributors can pick, pack and ship items in carefully measured quantities to customers, with the product’s movements throughout the supply chain, fully tracked and documented. And it is important to encourage the brand owners to take the necessary steps to create awareness to unleash their full potential.

# DrugTrace: Leveraging blockchain and IoT for anti-counterfeit drug traceability

Author links open overlay panelNouhailaEL Akramia, MohamedHaninea, AyoubHaratib, AminaMiskarb, ZinebELHallab, AyaEL Abidib, Waeal J.Obidallahc, HishamAlhulayyilc

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.softx.2026.102694](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.softx.2026.102694 "Persistent link using digital object identifier") [Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S235271102600186X&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/)

Open access

## Abstract

The pharmaceutical supply chain has long grappled with counterfeit drugs, cold-chain integrity failures, and transparency challenges, all of which compromise patient safety and public health. This paper introduces DrugTrace, a practical traceability platform that combines blockchain, IoT, and artificial intelligence (AI) technologies to address these gaps. The software integrates an Ethereum-based immutable ledger for end-to-end drug traceability, IoT sensors deployed via Node-RED to enable real-time environmental monitoring (temperature and humidity), and IPFS for off-chain data storage (sensor logs and certificates). A Random Forest classifier validates pharmaceutical marketing authorization (AMM) certificates, achieving 94% accuracy on a synthetic dataset of 1000 certificates. The proposed platform contributes to the development of secure and transparent supply chains in regulated industries beyond pharmaceuticals (AMM is the French acronym for Autorisation de Mise sur le Marché (Marketing Authorization)).

Packaging anti-counterfeiting and brand protection based on improved YOLOv8

## Article summary

Counterfeit packaging poses significant challenges in maintaining brand integrity, as traditional authentication methods often compromise visual appeal and detection accuracy. ## Abstract

Counterfeit packaging detection faces a critical dilemma in which rigorous authentication measures often compromise visual aesthetics, whereas unobtrusive micro-features frequently suffer from low detection rates due to their minute scale and background interference. To address this, we propose an enhanced YOLOv8 framework that harmonizes high-precision micro-feature recognition with computational efficiency suitable for edge deployment. Specifically, we introduce Mini-CBAM, a lightweight attention mechanism that substitutes standard multi-layer perceptrons with 1 × 1 convolutions to significantly reduce parameter redundancy while sharpening the focus on anti-counterfeiting regions. To mitigate the loss of texture details in deep convolutional networks, we design PA-FPN++, a multi-scale feature fusion architecture that establishes a high-resolution shortcut connection between shallow and deep layers. Furthermore, addressing the localization bias inherent in small target detection, we propose IoU-E Loss, a novel objective function incorporating an exponential penalty term to amplify gradient feedback during training. Complementing these architectural innovations, a GAN-based adversarial training strategy is employed to synthesize hard-negative samples, thereby enhancing model robustness against diverse forgery techniques. Extensive experiments on a multimodal dataset of 30,000 images demonstrate that our method achieves a mean Average Precision of 0.97, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in micro-feature recognition while maintaining real-time inference speeds.

## 1 Introduction

The proliferation of counterfeit product packaging presents a pervasive challenge in the global market, significantly undermining brand equity, jeopardizing consumer rights, and disrupting market stability. As illustrated in Fig. [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig1) (Left), the proportion of counterfeit packaging within the global illicit trade sector has exhibited a consistent upward trajectory, rising from 30.0% in 2019 to 35.0% in 2023. Given that the global counterfeit market reached an estimated valuation of $2.8 trillion in 2023, packaging accounts for a substantial share of this volume. High-value sectors, including luxury goods, pharmaceuticals, and premium spirits, are particularly vulnerable. For instance, a 2022 enforcement operation targeting counterfeit Moutai liquor in China resulted in the seizure of illicit products valued at over 1 billion RMB. These findings underscore the critical necessity for the development of efficient anti-counterfeiting technologies \[ [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR1 "El-Shafai W, Fouda MA, El-Rabaie EM, El-Salam NA. A comprehensive taxonomy on multimedia video forgery detection techniques: Challenges and novel trends. Multimed Tools Appl. 2024;83(2):4241–307.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s11042-023-15609-1                                    .")\].

Conventional anti-counterfeiting mechanisms, such as laser holograms and QR codes, exhibit inherent limitations. As illustrated in Fig. [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig2), these static features are increasingly susceptible to replication via sophisticated reproduction techniques. Furthermore, reconciling machine readability with design aesthetics presents a significant challenge; traditional labels often compromise the visual integrity of packaging, resulting in a suboptimal aesthetic score of approximately 3.2. Concurrently, detection accuracy is frequently compromised, falling below 85%. These deficiencies underscore the critical need for an interdisciplinary solution that integrates deep learning with design theory to simultaneously ensure technical robustness and aesthetic coherence \[ [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR2 "Xin Z, Chen S, Wu T, Shao Y, Ding W, You X. Few-shot object detection: Research advances and challenges. Inf Fusion. 2024;107:102307.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inffus.2024.102307                                    .")\].

Anti - Counterfeiting Technology Trade - off

This paper seeks to reconcile the dual challenges of optimizing technical performance and maintaining design integrity. From a technical standpoint, the objective is to achieve a detection accuracy of approximately 93% in brand packaging certification, with a processing latency not exceeding 0.5 s to satisfy the real-time demands of retail and logistics environments. Conversely, from a design perspective, the target is to attain an aesthetic satisfaction score surpassing 4.5 for anti-counterfeiting labels, a metric validated through quantitative surveys involving both designers and consumers. Methodologically, the research employs a systematic comparative approach, leveraging an enhanced YOLOv8 architecture to optimize micro-scale feature recognition \[ [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR3 "Guo Z, Zheng H, You C, Wang T, Liu C. DMF-Net: Dual-branch multi-scale feature fusion network for copy forgery identification of anti-counterfeiting QR code. arXiv. 2022.                    https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2201.07583                                    ")\], and establishes a comprehensive anti-counterfeiting dataset encompassing multi-brand, visible, and implicit features \[ [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR4 "Goodfellow IJ, Pouget-Abadie J, Mirza M, Xu B, Warde-Farley D, Ozair S, et al. Generative adversarial nets. Adv Neural Inf Process Syst. 2014;27:2672–80.")\]. Experimental validation rigorously assesses not only detection accuracy and inference speed but also the aesthetic coherence of the model’s output. By integrating principles from computer vision and design theory, this study proposes an “Anti-Counterfeiting - Aesthetic Balance Model.” Through multi-scale feature fusion and adaptive attention mechanisms, the model facilitates the collaborative detection of explicit and implicit elements \[ [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR5 "Zheng H, Zhou C, Li X, Wang T, You C. Forgery detection for anti-counterfeiting patterns using deep single classifier. Appl Sci. 2023;13(14):8101.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/app13148101                                    .")\]. Consequently, an intelligent solution is synthesized that harmonizes technical reliability with visual consistency \[ [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR6 "Daoud E, Vu D, Nguyen H, Gaedke M. Enhancing fake product detection using deep learning object detection models. IADIS Int J Comput Sci Inf Syst. 2020;15(1):13–24.                    https://doi.org/10.33965/ijcsis_2020150102                                    .")\]. The research framework is delineated in Fig. [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig3).

Research Framework: Limitations vs. Proposed Innovations

## 2 Materials and methods

### 2.1 Design of improved YOLOv8 anti-counterfeiting detection model​

Anti-counterfeiting packaging design represents an interdisciplinary convergence of engineering, materials science, and visual aesthetics. Its primary objective is to harmonize technical reliability with visual coherence, thereby preserving brand integrity while escalating the complexity required for illicit replication \[ [7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR7 "Soon JM, Manning L. Developing anti-counterfeiting measures: The role of smart packaging. Food Res Int. 2019;123:135–43.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodres.2019.04.049                                    ."), [67](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR67 "Zhang H, Hua D, Huang C, Samal SK, Xiong R, Sauvage F, et al. Materials and technologies to combat counterfeiting of pharmaceuticals: Current and future problem tackling. Adv Mater. 2020;32(11):1905486.                    https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201905486                                    .")\]. In practice, technical implementation strategies are categorized into three primary approaches. First, the utilization of covert, machine-readable security labels. For instance, Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) labels, generated via laser etching, exploit stochastic manufacturing processes to ensure non-replicability and certification reliability \[ [8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR8 "Sun N, Chen Z, Wang Y, Wang S, Xie Y, Liu Q. Random fractal-enabled physical unclonable functions with dynamic AI authentication. Nat Commun. 2023;14(1):2185.                    https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-37588-5                                    .")\]. Second, the leveraging of intrinsic material distinctiveness. Innovations such as embedding micro-diamonds into silk protein films enable the fabrication of flexible optical PUFs capable of adhering to complex geometries \[ [9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR9 "Hu YW, Zhang TP, Wang CF, Liu KK, Sun Y, Li L, et al. Flexible and biocompatible physical unclonable function anti-counterfeiting label. Adv Funct Mater. 2021;31(34):2102108.                    https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.202102108                                    .")\]. Third, the deployment of concealment and tamper-evident mechanisms. While UV-fluorescent inks are ubiquitous, their efficacy necessitates precise spectral calibration to ensure accurate identification. Industry case studies further validate these principles \[ [10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR10 "Cheng H, Wei X, Qiu H, Wang W, Su W, Zheng Y. Chemically stable fluorescent anti-counterfeiting labels achieved by UV-induced photolysis of nanocellulose. RSC Adv. 2021;11(30):18381–6.                    https://doi.org/10.1039/d1ra02089g                                    .")\]. Tiffany & Co. integrates chromatic authentication with brand recognition through its trademarked “Tiffany Blue” \[ [11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR11 "Aharon R, van der Horst FJD. Icons of change: Exploring the evolution of iconic assets in the luxury sector. Luxury. 2024. Available from:                    https://lup.lub.lu.se/luur/download?func=downloadFile&recordOId=9167568&fileOId=9167572                                    ")\], whereas Apple employs 0.08 mm laser micro-engraving to align with its minimalist ethos. Consequently, effective anti-counterfeiting design transcends mere technical utility, functioning as an integral extension of brand identity and aesthetic value.

To address the limitations of the standard YOLOv8 model in packaging anti-counterfeiting detection, specifically regarding suboptimal accuracy for small targets and instability within complex contexts, this study proposes an optimized architecture \[ [12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR12 "Khalili B, Smyth AW. SOD-YOLOv8—Enhancing YOLOv8 for small object detection in aerial imagery and traffic scenes. Sensors. 2024;24(19):6209.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/s24196209                                    ."), [13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR13 "Li Z, Wang Y, Zhang N, Zhang Y, Zhao Z, Xu D, et al. Deep learning-based object detection techniques for remote sensing images: A survey. Remote Sens. 2022;14(10):2385.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14102385                                    .")\]. The enhanced model integrates a lightweight attention mechanism to refine multi-scale feature extraction capabilities. Furthermore, adversarial training is employed to bolster model robustness and ensure stable performance in practical deployment. As illustrated in Fig. [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig4), the proposed Multi-Branch Anti-Counterfeiting YOLOv8 implements a specialized four-stage pipeline optimized for micro-feature detection. To enhance robustness against environmental interference, the network processes dual-source inputs consisting of real multimodal images and adversarial samples generated by StyleGAN3. Following feature extraction via a CSPDarknet-53 backbone, the data is fused within an enhanced PA-FPN + + neck. A critical innovation in this architecture is the Tiny Feature Shortcut, which mitigates the loss of 0.1 mm micro-details during deep down-sampling by routing high-resolution texture information directly through the Mini-CBAM attention module. Consequently, the prediction task is decoupled into three specialized heads. Head 1 targets explicit geometric features using the proposed IoU-E Loss, while Head 2 analyzes implicit material textures and Head 3 executes the final authenticity verification.

**Fig. 4**

![Fig. 4](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs42452-026-08572-7/MediaObjects/42452_2026_8572_Fig4_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7/figures/4)

The overall architecture of the model

### 2.2 Methods​

#### 2.2.1 Lightweight Attention Module (Mini-CBAM)​

While the standard CBAM module effectively highlights salient features, its heavy reliance on Multi-Layer Perceptrons introduces significant computational complexity \[ [14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR14 "Hoo SC, Ibrahim H, Suandi SA, Ng TF. Low-complexity attention module for lightweight face recognition networks. Mathematics. 2023;11(7):1694.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/math11071694                                    ."), [15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR15 "Mittal P. A comprehensive survey of deep learning-based lightweight object detection models for edge devices. Artif Intell Rev. 2024;57(9):242.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s10462-024-10877-1                                    ."), [19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR19 "Woo S, Park J, Lee JY, Kweon IS. CBAM: Convolutional block attention module. In: Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV). Munich, Germany. Springer; 2018. pp. 3–19.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-01234-2_1                                    ")\], rendering it unsuitable for resource-constrained mobile devices \[ [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR3 "Guo Z, Zheng H, You C, Wang T, Liu C. DMF-Net: Dual-branch multi-scale feature fusion network for copy forgery identification of anti-counterfeiting QR code. arXiv. 2022.                    https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2201.07583                                    ")\]. To resolve this bottleneck, we propose a lightweight variant, Mini-CBAM, designed to reduce parameter count while retaining attention efficacy. The detailed internal structure of the Mini-CBAM module is illustrated in Fig. [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig5). As depicted in the figure, the Mini-CBAM comprises two sequential sub-modules: the Channel Attention Module and the Spatial Attention Module. Specifically, to reduce model weight, we replace the computationally expensive fully connected layers in the original CBAM with \\(1\\times1\\) convolutions for channel compression. As shown in the yellow highlighted region of Fig. [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig5), the input feature map _F_ is first processed by global Average Pooling and Max Pooling. The aggregated features are then passed through a shared \\(1\\times1\\) convolution layer (with a compression ratio of 8) instead of an MLP. This modification reduces the parameter count by approximately 23% compared to the standard design \[ [17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR17 "Park W, Choi Y, Mekala MS, Choi GS, Yoo KY, Jung HY. A latency-efficient integration of channel attention for ConvNets. Comput Mater Contin. 2025;82(3):3965–81.                    https://doi.org/10.32604/cmc.2025.059966                                    ."), [18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR18 "Li Y, Zhang W, Liu Y, Shao X. A lightweight network for real-time smoke semantic segmentation based on dual paths. Neurocomputing. 2022;501:258–69.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neucom.2022.06.026                                    .")\]. Furthermore, in the spatial attention branch, we simplify the fusion strategy by concatenating the Average Pooling and Max Pooling features across the channel dimension, followed by a \\(3\\times3\\) convolution layer to generate the spatial attention map. The Mini-CBAM model can be mathematically represented in this way, and its calculation formula is as follows:

$${M}\_{c}\\left(F\\right)=\\sigma\\left(Con{v}\_{1\\times1}\\right(AvgPool\\left(F\\right))+Con{v}\_{1\\times1}(MaxPool\\left(F\\right)\\left)\\right)$$

(1)


where \\(\\sigma\\) denotes the Sigmoid activation function, and $F$ represents the input feature map. By integrating Mini-CBAM into the feature fusion neck of YOLOv8, the model dynamically boosts the response weight of high-frequency anti-counterfeiting regions, such as logo edges and micro-textures, while suppressing background interference \[ [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR20 "Liu S, Shao F, Chu W, Dai J, Zhang H. An improved YOLOv8-based lightweight attention mechanism for cross-scale feature fusion. Remote Sens. 2025;17(6):1044.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/rs17061044                                    ."), [21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR21 "Qu J, Tang Z, Zhang L, Zhang Y, Zhang Z. Remote sensing small object detection network based on attention mechanism and multi-scale feature fusion. Remote Sens. 2023;15(11):2728.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/rs15112728                                    ."), [22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR22 "Li M, Chen Y, Zhang T, Huang W. TA-YOLO: A lightweight small object detection model based on multi-dimensional trans-attention module for remote sensing images. Complex Intell Syst. 2024;10(4):5459–73.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s40747-024-01448-6                                    .")\]. Experimental results confirm that this lightweight design increases inference speed by 18% without compromising detection accuracy.

**Fig. 5**

![Fig. 5](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs42452-026-08572-7/MediaObjects/42452_2026_8572_Fig5_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7/figures/5)

The proposed Lightweight Attention Module

#### 2.2.2 Multi-scale feature fusion enhancement (PA-FPN++)

In the task of packaging anti-counterfeiting detection, critical features such as laser etchings and micro-QR codes are often minute (approx. 0.1 mm) \[ [23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR23 "Lin TY, Dollár P, Girshick R, He K, Hariharan B, Belongie S. Feature pyramid networks for object detection. In: Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). Honolulu, HI, USA. IEEE; 2017. pp. 936–944.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/CVPR.2017.106                                    "), [24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR24 "Huang W, Li G, Chen Q, Ju M, Qu J. CF2PN: A cross-scale feature fusion pyramid network based remote sensing target detection. Remote Sens. 2021;13(5):847.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/rs13050847                                    .")\] and submerged in complex background textures \[ [25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR25 "Chen Y, Zhu X, Li Y, Wei Y, Ye L. Enhanced semantic feature pyramid network for small object detection. Signal Process Image Commun. 2023;113:116919.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.image.2023.116919                                    .")\]. Traditional Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN) typically downsample features to 1/8 or 1/32 scales, causing the permanent loss of such high-frequency details. To address this, we propose an enhanced architecture, PA-FPN++, which introduces a novel cross-scale feature injection mechanism. The detailed schematic of the PA-FPN + + feature fusion process is illustrated in Fig. [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig6). As depicted in the figure, unlike standard YOLOv8 which discards shallow features, we establish a specialized “Tiny Feature Shortcut” (highlighted in the orange path). This mechanism directly injects high-resolution features from the backbone’s _C_ _2_ layer (1/4 scale) into the fusion network to construct a new high-resolution prediction layer _p_ _2_. This design explicitly prevents the washing out of micro-textures during deep convolution \[ [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR20 "Liu S, Shao F, Chu W, Dai J, Zhang H. An improved YOLOv8-based lightweight attention mechanism for cross-scale feature fusion. Remote Sens. 2025;17(6):1044.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/rs17061044                                    ."), [21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR21 "Qu J, Tang Z, Zhang L, Zhang Y, Zhang Z. Remote sensing small object detection network based on attention mechanism and multi-scale feature fusion. Remote Sens. 2023;15(11):2728.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/rs15112728                                    ."), [22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR22 "Li M, Chen Y, Zhang T, Huang W. $$\\begin{array}{ll}&{p}\_{5}={C}\_{5}\\\&{p}\_{4}=Upsample\\left({p}\_{5}\\right)\\oplus{C}\_{4}\\\&{p}\_{3}=Upsample\\left({p}\_{4}\\right)\\oplus{C}\_{3}\\\&{p}\_{2}=Upsample\\left({p}\_{3}\\right)\\oplus{C}\_{2}\\oplus Lateral({C}\_{2},{C}\_{3})\\\&{n}\_{2}=CBAM\\left({p}\_{2}\\right)\\\&{n}\_{3}=CBAM\\left(Downsample\\right({n}\_{2})\\oplus{p}\_{3})\\\&{n}\_{4}=CBAM\\left(Downsample\\right({n}\_{3})\\oplus{p}\_{4})\\\&{n}\_{5}=CBAM\\left(Downsample\\right({n}\_{4})\\oplus{p}\_{5})\\end{array}$$

where \\(\\oplus\\) denotes element-wise summation, _C_ _i_ represents the backbone features, and _n_ _i_ denotes the final output features for the detection heads.

#### 2.2.3 Loss function optimization (IoU-E Loss)​

In the context of brand packaging anti-counterfeiting, micro-scale features (e.g., 0.1 mm laser etchings) exhibit extremely limited spatial support. This characteristic poses a fundamental challenge for traditional IoU-based loss functions, which often suffer from insufficient gradient magnitude when the predicted box significantly deviates from the ground truth. To bridge this gap, we propose the IoU-E Loss, which incorporates an exponential penalty term to reshape the gradient landscape and heighten model sensitivity to micro-targets:

$${L}\_{IoU-E}=1-CIoU+\\alpha\\cdot{e}^{-\\gamma\\cdot IoU}$$

(3)


where \\(C\\) represents the diagonal length of the smallest enclosing rectangle, while \\({\\upalpha}\\) and \\({\\upgamma}\\) are hyperparameters governing the penalty intensity. To theoretically justify the efficacy of this formulation, we propose the following:

### Proposition 1

The proposed IoU-E Loss induces non-linear gradient amplification in low-overlap regions, effectively mitigating the gradient attenuation and localization bias inherent in micro-object detection.

### Proof

To analyze the contribution to the regression process, we derive the gradient of \\({L}\_{IoU-E}\\) with respect to the IoU as follows:

$$\\begin{aligned}{\\nabla}\_{\\text{x}}{\\text{L}}\_{\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U}-\\text{E}}&=\\frac{\\partial}{\\partial\\text{x}}\\left\[1-\\text{C}\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U}+{\\upalpha}\\cdot{\\text{e}}^{-{\\upgamma}\\cdot\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U}}\\right\]\\\&=\\frac{\\partial(1-\\text{C}\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U})}{\\partial\\text{x}}+{\\upalpha}\\cdot\\frac{\\partial{\\text{e}}^{-{\\upgamma}\\cdot\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U}}}{\\partial\\text{x}}\\\&=-\\frac{\\partial\\text{C}\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U}}{\\partial\\text{x}}-{\\upalpha}{\\upgamma}{\\text{e}}^{-{\\upgamma}\\cdot\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U}}\\frac{\\partial\\text{I}\\text{o}\\text{U}}{\\partial\\text{x}}\\end{aligned}$$

(4)


The term \\(\\alpha\\gamma{e}^{-\\gamma\\cdot IoU}\\) functions as a dynamic gain coefficient, providing a strong impetus for the model to refine bounding box positions \[ [38](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR38 "Yao Y, Cheng G, Wang G, Li S, Zhou P, Xie X. On improving bounding box representations for oriented object detection. IEEE Trans Geosci Remote Sens. 2023;61:1–11.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/TGRS.2022.3231340                                    .")\]. Introducing this exponential penalty renders the network significantly more sensitive to small targets with large errors \[ [39](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR39 "Iyengar G, Sigman K. Exponential penalty function control of loss networks. Ann Appl Probab. 2004;14(4):1698–740.                    https://doi.org/10.1214/105051604000000936                                    .")\]. Specifically, when the target IoU is relatively small (e.g., < 0.5) \[ [40](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR40 "Yan J, Wang H, Yan M, Diao W, Sun X, Li H. IoU-adaptive deformable R-CNN: Make full use of IoU for multi-class object detection in remote sensing imagery. Remote Sens. 2019;11(3):286.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/rs11030286                                    .")\], standard loss functions fail to impose sufficient penalties \[ [31](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR31 "Cai D, Zhang Z, Zhang Z. Corner-point and foreground-area IoU loss: Better localization of small objects in bounding box regression. Sensors. 2023;23(10):4961.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/s23104961                                    ."), [35](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR35 "Pang J, Chen K, Shi J, Feng H, Ouyang W, Lin D, Libra. R-CNN: Towards balanced learning for object detection. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). Long Beach, CA, USA. IEEE; 2019. pp. 821–830.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/CVPR.2019.00091                                    "), [36](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR36 "Zhai H, Cheng J, Wang M. Rethink the IoU-based loss functions for bounding box regression. In: 2020 IEEE 9th Joint International Information Technology and Artificial Intelligence Conference (ITAIC). Chongqing, China. IEEE; 2020. pp. 1522–1528.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/ITAIC49862.2020.9339070                                    "), [41](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR41 "Tian D, Han Y, Wang S, Chen X, Guan T. Absolute size IoU loss for the bounding box regression of the object detection. Neurocomputing. 2022;500:1029–40.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neucom.2022.06.018                                    .")\]. In small object detection, due to the limited pixels occupied by the target, traditional loss functions may encounter the problem of vanishing gradients, leading to a slow training process or even stagnation. In contrast, the IoU-E loss leverages the exponential term to strictly magnify these discrepancies \[ [42](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR42 "Liu B, Jiang W. RS-YOLOx: Target feature enhancement and bounding box auxiliary regression based object detection approach for remote sensing. J Appl Remote Sens. 2024;18(1):016514.                    https://doi.org/10.1117/1.JRS.18.016514                                    .")\], thereby providing a stronger gradient signal for small objects and alleviating the issue of vanishing gradients. This exponential loss function sharply increases the loss value when the IoU between prediction and ground truth remains at a low level \[ [43](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR43 "Chen Z, Lin Y, Xu J, Lu K, Huang Z. A fused score computation approach to reflect the overlap between the predicted box and the ground truth in pedestrian detection. IET Image Process. 2024;18(13):4287–96.                    https://doi.org/10.1049/ipr2.13251                                    .")\], forcing the model to adjust the bounding box position, effectively correcting low-accuracy predictions and reducing localization bias \[ [44](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR44 "Zhang YF, Ren W, Zhang Z, Jia Z, Wang L, Tan T. Focal and efficient IOU loss for accurate bounding box regression. Neurocomputing. 2022;506:146–57.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neucom.2022.07.042                                    ."), [45](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR45 "Corbière C, Thome N, Bar-Hen A, Cord M, Pérez P. Addressing failure prediction by learning model confidence. Adv Neural Inf Process Syst. 2019;32:2898–909.")\]. Conversely, as the model achieves high-precision alignment, the penalty term decays to prevent overfitting. Experimental results confirm that this method boosts small target mAP@0.5 by 6.2%.

#### 2.2.4 Anti-counterfeiting feature extraction and encoding​

We propose a hierarchical feature extraction framework designed to distinguish between explicit and implicit anti-counterfeiting characteristics. Explicit feature analysis targets logo contours by utilizing Canny edge detection to identify 16 key coordinate points. This stage also assesses the periodicity of patterns, such as the Gucci double G logo, and validates the geometric precision of the design.

Implicit features comprise a 256-dimensional LBP texture histogram of the material surface and a spectral reflectance curve in the 940 nm near-infrared band containing 128 sampling points. These attributes serve to discriminate between the specific ink formulations and paper substrates used in authentic versus counterfeit packaging \[ [37](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR37 "Wang Y, Wang B, Huo L, Fan Y. GT-YOLO: Nearshore infrared ship detection based on infrared images. J Mar Sci Eng. 2024;12(2):213.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/jmse12020213                                    ."), [46](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR46 "Dégardin K, Guillemain A, Klespe P, Hindelang F, Zurbach R, Roggo Y. Packaging analysis of counterfeit medicines. Forensic Sci Int. 2018;291:144–57.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.forsciint.2018.08.023                                    .")\]. By systematically analyzing surface texture characteristics and recording near-infrared reflectivity, this method captures subtle material properties that are typically imperceptible to the naked eye. To address the challenges associated with directly observing these micro-textures and spectral variations, we propose a specialized coding technique.

#### 2.2.5 Adversarial training for enhanced robustness​

To enhance model robustness in complex scenarios characterized by lighting variations, printing defects, and forged variants, this study constructs an adversarial training framework based on StyleGAN3 \[ [47](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR47 "Rössler A, Cozzolino D, Verdoliva L, Riess C, Thies J, Nießner M. FaceForensics++: Learning to detect manipulated facial images. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV). Seoul, Korea. IEEE; 2019. pp. 1–11.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCV.2019.00009                                    ")\]. Within this framework, the generator leverages style vector adjustments to synthesize high-quality counterfeit packaging samples that simulate six distinct interference effects. These perturbations include light intensity variations, Gaussian blur, print color deviation, micro-texture tampering, crease occlusion, and the blending of forged features \[ [48](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR48 "Zhou P, Han X, Morariu VI, Davis LS. Learning rich features for image manipulation detection. In: Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). Salt Lake City, UT, USA. IEEE; 2018. pp. 1053–1061.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/CVPR.2018.00116                                    ")\]. The incorporation of these diverse interference samples significantly augments the discriminative capability of the model against heterogeneous counterfeits. During the adversarial training process, the discriminator employs the improved YOLOv8 architecture to execute a dual function. It simultaneously performs object detection by localizing targets and identifying categories while evaluating sample authenticity to output a credibility score \[ [61](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR61 "Lin TY, Goyal P, Girshick R, He K, Dollár P. Focal loss for dense object detection. In: Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV). Venice, Italy. IEEE; 2017. pp. 2980–2988.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCV.2017.324                                    ")\]. The total loss function is formulated as:​

$${L}\_{total}={L}\_{cls}+{L}\_{IoU-E}+\\lambda\\cdot{L}\_{adv}$$

(5)


where \\({L}\_{cls}\\) represents the classification loss. The adversarial loss is defined as:

$${L}\_{adv}=-{E}\_{x\\sim{p}\_{real}}\\left\[\\text{log}D\\right(x\\left)\\right\]-{E}\_{z\\sim{p}\_{z}}\[\\text{log}(1-D(G\\left(z\\right)\\left)\\right)\]$$

(6)


and \\({\\uplambda}=0.3\\) serves as the balance parameter \[ [49](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR49 "Yang Y, Yang Y, Zhou L, Zou J. A lightweight multi-task learning network based on key area guidance for counterfeit detection. Signal Image Video Process. 2024;18(5):4675–85.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s11760-024-03105-4                                    .")\].

### 2.3 Training strategy and parameter settings​

The model training protocol is structured into three distinct phases spanning a total of 300 epochs. Phase I involves pre-training on the COCO dataset to establish foundational object detection capabilities \[ [52](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR52 "Wang W, Lai Q, Fu H, Shen J, Ling H. Salient object detection in the deep learning era: An in-depth survey. IEEE Trans Pattern Anal Mach Intell. 2021;44(6):3239–59.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/TPAMI.2021.3051099                                    .")\]. In this stage, the model is initialized with improved YOLOv8 weights and optimized using Stochastic Gradient Descent with a momentum of 0.9 and an initial learning rate of 0.01 \[ [50](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR50 "Saeheaw T, SC-YOLO. A real-time CSP-based YOLOv11n variant optimized with Sophia for accurate PPE detection on construction sites. Buildings. 2025;15(16):2854.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/buildings15162854                                    ."), [51](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR51 "Cutkosky A, Mehta H. Momentum improves normalized SGD. In: Proceedings of the 37th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML). Virtual. PMLR; 2020. pp. 2260–2268.")\]. Phase II focuses on fine-tuning using a proprietary dataset comprising 200,000 authentic and counterfeit packaging samples \[ [53](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR53 "He Z, Yang J, Ning H, Li C, Tang Q. DBA-YOLO: A dense target detection model based on lightweight neural networks. J Imaging. 2025;11(10):345.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/jimaging11100345                                    .")\]. During this phase, the learning rate decays to \\(1{0}^{-4}\\), and data augmentation techniques, including random cropping and color jittering, are employed to enhance model generalization and robustness \[ [54](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR54 "Chamarty A. Fine-tuning of learning rate for improvement of object detection accuracy. In: 2020 IEEE India Council International Subsections Conference (INDISCON). Visakhapatnam, India. IEEE; 2020. pp. 135–141.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/INDISCON50162.2020.00038                                    "), [55](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR55 "Pei X, Zhao YH, Chen L, Guo Q, Duan Z, Pan Y, et al. Robustness of machine learning to color, size change, normalization, and image enhancement on micrograph datasets with large sample differences. Mater Des. 2023;232:112086.                    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2023.112086                                    .")\]. Phase III implements adversarial training by introducing 50,000 interference samples synthesized via a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) to bolster resilience against adversarial attacks \[ [56](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR56 "Wang C, Xu C, Yao X, Tao D. Evolutionary generative adversarial networks. IEEE Trans Evol Comput. 2019;23(6):921–34.                    https://doi.org/10.1109/TEVC.2019.2895748                                    .")\]. In this final stage, the learning rate is further reduced to \\(1{0}^{-5}\\), and generator weights are updated every 5 epochs \[ [57](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR57 "Moreira M, Fiesler E. Neural networks with adaptive learning rate and momentum terms. Idiap Res Inst Tech Rep. 1995;95(4):1–29.")\]. All experiments are conducted on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU with a batch size of 32 and a fixed input resolution of 640 × 640.

### 2.4 Construction of anti-counterfeiting packaging database​

#### 2.4.1 Core Experimental brands and their signature features​

This study focuses on three distinct brands: Moutai, Apple, and Chanel. For Moutai (baijiu), anti-counterfeiting analysis centers on bottle cap inkjet coding, UV fluorescent patterns, and metal cap reflectivity. In counterfeit samples, inkjet coding exhibits inconsistent font thickness exceeding \\(\\pm0.02\\) mm compared to the standard \\(0.05\\pm0.01\\) mm, along with uneven character spacing greater than \\(\\pm0.03\\)mm relative to the \\(0.3\\pm0.02\\) mm benchmark. UV fluorescent patterns in fake products often display weak or absent blue-green fluorescence, with a peak wavelength shift exceeding \\(\\pm20\\) nm from the authentic 450 nm peak under 365 nm UV light. Additionally, counterfeit metal caps typically demonstrate reflectivity below 0.5 under a 650 nm laser, significantly lower than the genuine standard of \\(0.7\\pm0.05\\), and feature irregular reflection patterns.Apple electronic products are examined based on box seal laser-etched microtextures and laser-engraved serial numbers. Counterfeit microtextures are characterized by shallow etching depths of 0.05–0.07 mm, deviating from the authentic range of \\(0.08\\pm0.01\\) mm, and possess uneven textures. Regarding serial numbers, counterfeit engravings display smooth edges with a fractal dimension below 1.1 and inconsistent line widths, whereas genuine engravings maintain an edge fractal dimension of \\(1.3\\pm0.1\\).Chanel anti-counterfeiting features primarily involve the curvature of the double C logo and wrapping paper embossing. Counterfeit logos exhibit significant geometric deviations, including arc asymmetry and an inner radius error exceeding 0.2 mm compared to the standard \\(2.5\\pm0.02\\) mm. Similarly, counterfeit wrapping paper embossing is identifiable by a shallow depth of 0.08–0.12 mm, contrasting with the genuine specification of \\(0.15\\pm0.02\\) mm, and a generally blurred appearance.

#### 2.4.2 Data acquisition protocols​

The total sample size _T_ for a given brand is calculated, where _B_ represents the number of batches, _S_ denotes the number of samples per batch, and _F_ indicates the count of counterfeit specimens.

$$T=(B\\times S)+F$$

(7)


Beyond sample size determination, the image acquisition protocol employs three distinct light sources to ensure comprehensive feature extraction. These illumination modalities include RGB at 5500 K daylight, Near-Infrared (NIR) using an 850 nm filter, and Ultraviolet (UV) at 365 nm. Regarding hardware configuration, RGB images are captured using a Canon EOS R5 (24MP) paired with a 100 mm macro lens. For spectral imaging, a modified Sony A7R IV is utilized to acquire NIR data, while a Basler acA2500-14uc camera is dedicated to UV image capture. Finally, environmental conditions are strictly standardized. The dataset’s authenticity and applicability are significantly influenced by the source of the counterfeit samples. In this study, counterfeit samples were sourced from actual counterfeit products seized during anti-counterfeiting operations, reflecting real-world counterfeiting practices. These samples were not generated via Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) or simulated in laboratories, ensuring their relevance. This approach enhances the model’s robustness and generalizability, as the variety of counterfeiting methods strengthens its ability to detect and classify counterfeit products under diverse conditions. This involves controlling indoor lighting stability and maintaining a fixed working distance of 30 cm between the camera sensor and the sample surface.

#### 2.4.3 Data augmentation strategies​

The data augmentation pipeline constructs a multi-dimensional framework by integrating geometric transformations, lighting simulations, and noise injection. Geometric operations include rotation within \\(\\pm3{0}^{\\circ}\\), scaling between 0.8× and 1.2×, and shearing angles of \\(\\pm1{0}^{\\circ}\\). To enhance robustness against variable illumination, the system simulates diverse indoor and outdoor environments through brightness and contrast adjustments of \\(\\pm30\\%\\) and \\(\\pm20\\%\\) respectively, alongside shadow injection with opacity ranging from 20% to 40%. Noise injection techniques are employed to replicate real-world image degradation. Specifically, Gaussian blur with kernel sizes from 1 × 1 to 5 × 5, salt-and-pepper noise with densities of 0.01 to 0.05, and JPEG compression quality between 50 and 90 are applied. These artifacts simulate motion blur, low-light sensor noise, and transmission losses, thereby ensuring stable model performance in complex environments.

#### 2.4.4 Data annotation standards​

A standardized annotation framework was established by integrating manual labeling with semi-automatic tools, specifically Label Studio and VGG Image Annotator \[ [58](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR58 "Aljabri M, AlAmir M, AlGhamdi M, Abdel-Mottaleb M, Collado-Mesa F. Towards a better understanding of annotation tools for medical imaging: A survey. Multimed Tools Appl. 2022;81(18):25877–911.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s11042-022-12100-1                                    .")\]. Logo annotation employed Label Studio to generate polygonal outlines and precise angle measurements. This process captured critical parameters including contour coordinates and intersection angles, such as the 60\\(\\pm\\)2° angle in LV logos, ensuring both consistency and accuracy. For microstructure annotation, VGG Image Annotator was utilized to define bounding boxes and pixel masks, facilitating the analysis of size, spacing, and gray values. Additionally, material texture annotation relied on custom Python scripts to perform ROI masking and feature extraction for metrics such as LBP histograms and fractal dimensions. This rigorous protocol culminated in the creation of a dataset exceeding 30,000 images, which comprises 1,000 samples across three brands with 10 augmentations per sample. As illustrated in Fig. [7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig7), the collection and augmentation procedures adhere to strict standards to simulate real-world scenarios.

#### 2.4.5 Experimental environment and software configuration

The graphics card uses NVIDIA RTX 4090, which is 24GB, and the graphics card is much better than the previous version, and the memory bandwidth is larger, and the memory bandwidth is larger, and the model can be trained and reasoned at the same time. The CPU is Intel Xeon E5-2690 v4, with 14 core 28 threads, the frequency is 2.6 GHz, which is mainly used to process data and operating systems \[ [59](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR59 "Wakonig K, Stadler HC, Odstrčil M, Tsai EHR, Diaz A, Holler M, et al. PtychoShelves, a versatile high-level framework for high-performance analysis of ptychographic data. J Appl Crystallogr. 2020;53(2):574–86.                    https://doi.org/10.1107/S1600576720001776                                    .")\]. GPU computing is also accelerated with CUDA 11.7. The model is trained with a mixed precision FP16, which allows the memory to occupy less than half and the training speed is almost 30% faster.

#### 2.4.6 Baseline models

Although newer iterations such as YOLOv10 and YOLOv11 have recently been introduced, this study selects YOLOv8 as the foundational baseline for two strategic reasons rooted in industrial applicability. First, YOLOv8 offers a verified balance between inference speed and feature retention capability, whereas recent studies indicate that newer models optimizing aggressively for latency often sacrifice the high-frequency feature resolution required for detecting micro-defects \[ [60](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR60 "Hussain M. YOLO-v1 to YOLO-v8, the rise of YOLO and its complementary nature toward digital manufacturing and industrial defect detection. Machines. 2023;11(7):677.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/machines11070677                                    ."), [76](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR76 "Bento J, Paixão T, Alvarez AB. Performance Evaluation of YOLOv8, YOLOv9, YOLOv10, and YOLOv11 for Stamp Detection in Scanned Documents. Appl Sci. 2025;15(6):3154.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/app15063154                                    .")\]. Second, YOLOv8 possesses the most mature industrial deployment ecosystem, including stable support for ONNX Runtime and TensorRT, which is critical for the practical edge application proposed in this paper \[ [77](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR77 "Reis D, Kupec J, Hong J, Daoudi A. Real-Time Flying Object Detection with YOLOv8. arXiv. 2023.                    https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2305.09972                                    ")\]. To comprehensively evaluate performance, we selected three representative models for comparison, including the original YOLOv8 as the primary anchor to quantify improvements, YOLOv7 for its documented proficiency in small object detection tasks, and Faster R-CNN as a high-precision benchmark despite its higher computational cost. All models were trained for 300 epochs on the anti-counterfeiting packaging database with a batch size of 32 to ensure strict experimental consistency.

#### 2.4.7 Evaluation metrics

Using a multidimensional evaluation framework, a comprehensive evaluation of model performance, covering detection accuracy, anti-counterfeiting specificity, real-time efficiency:

1. 1.
Detection Performance Metrics: _Metrics Mean Average Precision_ (mAP@0.5:0.95) represents the average precision calculated across Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds ranging from 0.5 to 0.95 in increments of 0.05. This metric evaluates the model’s robustness in detecting objects with varying degrees of overlap relative to ground truth \[ [11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR11 "Aharon R, van der Horst FJD. Icons of change: Exploring the evolution of iconic assets in the luxury sector. Luxury. 2024. Available from:                    https://lup.lub.lu.se/luur/download?func=downloadFile&recordOId=9167568&fileOId=9167572                                    ")\]. _Precision_ is defined as the ratio of true positive (TP) detections to the total number of positive predictions (TP + False Positives \[FP\]), quantifying the capability to minimize false positives. _Recall_ denotes the ratio of true positives to the total number of actual positive instances (TP + False Negatives \[FN\]), measuring the capacity to identify all authentic anti-counterfeiting features.

2. 2.
Anti-Counterfeiting Specificity Metrics: _False Acceptance Rate_ (FAR) indicates the percentage of counterfeit samples incorrectly classified as genuine. It is calculated as FP/(FP + TN) where TN represents true negatives, and minimizing this metric is critical for mitigating brand risk. _False Rejection Rate_ (FRR) represents the percentage of authentic samples incorrectly classified as counterfeit, defined as FN/(FN + TP). Low FRR ensures that legitimate products are not erroneously rejected \[ [62](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR62 "Zeng Y, Guo D, He W, Zhang T, Liu Z. ARF-YOLOv8: A novel real-time object detection model for UAV-captured images detection. J Real-Time Image Process. 2024;21(4):107.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s11554-024-01483-z                                    .")\].

3. 3.
Real-Time Efficiency Metrics: _Inference Time_ measures the latency required to process a single 640 × 640 pixel image on a mobile device. Expressed in milliseconds, this metric directly determines the feasibility of mobile deployment \[ [63](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR63 "Benatia MA, Baudry D, Louis A. Detecting counterfeit products by means of frequent pattern mining. J Ambient Intell Humaniz Comput. 2022;13(7):3683–92.                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s12652-020-02237-y                                    ."), [64](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR64 "Tahilramani N, Ahir P, Saxena S, Talreja VP, Charanarur P. Edge-based AI solution for enhancing urban safety: Helmet compliance monitoring with YOLOv9 on Raspberry Pi. Discov Internet Things. 2025;5(25).                    https://doi.org/10.1007/s43926-025-00113-9                                    ."), [65](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR65 "Fuhad KF, Tuba JF, Sarker MRA, Momen S, Mohammed N, Rahman T. Deep learning based automatic malaria parasite detection from blood smear and its smartphone based application. Diagnostics. 2020;10(5):329.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/diagnostics10050329                                    .")\]. _Frames Per Second_ (FPS) quantifies processing throughput and is calculated as 1000 divided by the inference time. This value reflects the model’s suitability for real-time applications \[ [12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR12 "Khalili B, Smyth AW. SOD-YOLOv8—Enhancing YOLOv8 for small object detection in aerial imagery and traffic scenes. Sensors. 2024;24(19):6209.                    https://doi.org/10.3390/s24196209                                    .")\].


## 3 Results

### 3.1 Cross-model performance comparison

This paper aims to comprehensively verify the performance of the improved YOLOv8. Comparative experiments were conducted with the most advanced baseline model, and ablation studies were also carried out to quantify the role of each key component. The training dynamics and final metrics are as shown in Fig. [8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig8).

Figure [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig6) illustrates the training trajectory of the improved YOLOv8 model. The data exhibits a consistent decline in loss values inversely correlated with a steady ascent in Mean Average Precision (mAP), Precision, and Recall. Concurrently, the error metrics, specifically the False Acceptance Rate (FAR) and False Rejection Rate (FRR), demonstrate a continuous reduction. These optimization trends, observed over 300 epochs, confirm that the model achieves stable convergence and robust overall performance.

The improved framework secures a 5% gain in mAP@0.5:0.95 relative to the standard YOLOv8. Precision and recall metrics both surpass 0.98, confirming the model’s efficacy in identifying visible and hidden security features. Additionally, the error rates are minimized, with FAR dropping to 0.8% and FRR to 0.5%. To validate real-world applicability, the proposed method is evaluated using Maotai packaging, which incorporates complex anti-counterfeiting mechanisms such as UV fluorescence and reflective metal caps \[ [13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#ref-CR13 "Li Z, Wang Y, Zhang N, Zhang Y, Zhao Z, Xu D, et al. Table [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Tab2) illustrates that the improved model exhibits exceptional efficacy in micro-feature detection. By leveraging the Mini-CBAM module to focus on minute textural changes, the system successfully resolves 0.1 mm spray-printed codes and UV patterns previously undetectable by the original YOLOv8, achieving a 98.2% accuracy rate. Simultaneously, the computational efficiency is improved, reducing inference time from 450 to 320 milliseconds. This acceleration results from the lightweight architecture of the Mini-CBAM, which eliminates unnecessary computational redundancy.

### 3.3 Ablation studies

As evidenced in Table [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Tab3), the baseline YOLOv8 achieves a mAP@0.5:0.95 of 0.92 with a False Acceptance Rate (FAR) of 3.1%. The integration of individual components yields distinct performance gains. Specifically, the Mini-CBAM module enhances feature extraction, raising the mAP to 0.95, while Adversarial Training proves critical for security specificity by significantly reducing the FAR to 1.2%. The fully integrated model, designated as the Improved YOLOv8, synergizes these enhancements to attain a peak mAP of 0.97 and a minimal FAR of 0.8%. Furthermore, the architectural optimizations result in an inference time of 320 ms, demonstrating that the proposed framework achieves superior accuracy and robustness without compromising computational efficiency.

**Table 3 Impact of Individual Components on Anti-Counterfeiting Detection Performance**

[Full size table](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7/tables/3)

Figure [10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig10) visually delineates the individual contributions of each architectural component. Quantitative analysis indicates that the Mini-CBAM module serves as the primary driver for precision, yielding a 3% increase in mAP. Conversely, adversarial training is instrumental in minimizing security risks, reducing the False Acceptance Rate (FAR) by 1.9%. Furthermore, the integration of PA-FPN + + and IoU-E Loss collectively enhances detection accuracy for small objects by 4.2%, specifically improving the recognition of 0.1 mm-scale micro-QR codes and laser etchings. The synergistic combination of these elements results in the superior overall performance of the full model, while parameter pruning within the Mini-CBAM architecture ensures optimized inference latency.

### 3.4 Comparison with latest YOLO series (v10/v11/v12)

To assess the competitiveness of the proposed architecture against state-of-the-art object detection models, we benchmark the Improved-YOLOv8 against the official YOLOv10n, YOLOv11n, and the latest YOLOv12n using the anti-counterfeiting dataset. Table [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Tab4) summarizes the key performance metrics following a 300-epoch fine-tuning process conducted with identical hyperparameters.

**Table 4 Anti-Counterfeiting Performance vs. Latest YOLOs**

[Full size table](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7/tables/4)

While YOLOv12 demonstrates superior architectural advancements on the general COCO validation set by achieving a mAP@0.5:0.95 of 0.54 compared to 0.51 for YOLOv8, it faces limitations in specific anti-counterfeiting tasks. The deeper backbone of YOLOv12 tends to over-smooth 0.1 mm-level micro-features, resulting in a mAP\_small that is 3% lower than the proposed model. To address this, integrating the Mini-CBAM module into the YOLOv12 architecture, denoted as YOLOv12 + Mini-CBAM, elevates mAP\_small from 0.89 to 0.91 with a marginal latency increase of 7 ms. This improvement highlights the modularity and transferability of the contribution. Furthermore, Fig. [11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig11) visualizes the efficiency-accuracy Pareto frontier to illustrate the trade-off analysis.

### 3.5 Comparison with cross-model performance

In this section, the study compares the Enhanced YOLOv8 model and the YOLOv8 Nano model, examining detection accuracy (mAP), inference time, and frames per second (FPS) to demonstrate the strengths and weaknesses of each model. To further explore the feasibility of model deployment and analyse practical application potential on edge devices, this study additionally compares the computational resource consumption differences between the two models, encompassing floating-point operations per second (FLOPs), model parameter count, and inference time. Table [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Tab5) presents a comparative analysis of computational resource consumption between the Enhanced YOLOv8 and YOLOv8 Nano models.

**Table 5 Comparison of computational resource consumption between the models**

[Full size table](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7/tables/5)

The results in Table [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Tab5) demonstrate that the enhanced YOLOv8 exhibits outstanding accuracy but consumes higher computational resources, whereas YOLOv8 Nano possesses distinct advantages in real-time performance and computational efficiency.

In terms of floating-point operations per second (FLOPs), the enhanced YOLOv8 requires approximately 250 million operations, whereas YOLOv8 Nano requires only 120 million, representing a reduction of approximately 50% in computational load. Regarding model parameters, the enhanced YOLOv8 has 60 million parameters, while YOLOv8 Nano has 25 million, a reduction of approximately 60%. In terms of inference time, the enhanced YOLOv8 achieves 320 milliseconds, whereas YOLOv8 Nano completes the task in just 200 milliseconds, demonstrating superior real-time performance. Figure [12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-026-08572-7#Fig12) illustrates the computational resource consumption comparison between the two models, highlighting YOLOv8 Nano’s suitability for edge devices and mobile applications, while the enhanced YOLOv8 remains appropriate for scenarios demanding higher accuracy.
